# Standard Frontline Treatment: R-CHOP and Beyond

## The R-CHOP Backbone

Rituximab combined with cyclophosphamide, doxorubicin, vincristine, and prednisone (R-CHOP) has remained the standard frontline therapy for DLBCL for over two decades. The regimen consists of rituximab 375 mg/m^2^, cyclophosphamide 750 mg/m^2^, doxorubicin 50 mg/m^2^, and vincristine 1.4 mg/m^2^ (capped at 2 mg) administered intravenously on day 1, plus prednisone 100 mg orally on days 1--5, repeated every 21 days for six to eight cycles [@Li2018Diffuse; @SusanibarAdaniya2021]. The addition of rituximab to CHOP chemotherapy represented a landmark advance, improving cure rates substantially. However, despite this progress, approximately 30--40% of patients are not cured with R-CHOP. Five-year overall survival (OS) rates range from 60% to 65%, with progression-free survival (PFS) of approximately 55--60%, and up to 50% of patients ultimately become refractory to or relapse after treatment [@Crump2017Outcomes; @SusanibarAdaniya2021]. Patients who achieve event-free survival at 24 months (EFS24) have near-normal life expectancy, with minimal loss of lifetime compared with age-matched controls [@Jakobsen2017Minimal]. This observation underscores the importance of achieving durable first remission.

Outcomes with R-CHOP vary substantially by risk group. In the SCHOLAR-1 retrospective analysis of 636 patients with refractory DLBCL, the objective response rate to next-line therapy was only 26% (complete response rate, 7%), with a median OS of 6.3 months and only 20% alive at 2 years [@Crump2017Outcomes]. These sobering figures highlight the critical need for effective frontline therapy and the poor salvageability of primary refractory disease.

## Attempts to Improve Upon R-CHOP

Multiple strategies have been evaluated to improve upon R-CHOP in the frontline setting, with largely disappointing results. The Alliance/CALGB 50303 phase III intergroup trial compared dose-adjusted EPOCH-R (etoposide, prednisone, vincristine, cyclophosphamide, doxorubicin, and rituximab) with standard R-CHOP in 491 evaluable patients [@Bartlett2019Doseadjusted]. At a median follow-up of 5 years, PFS was not statistically different between arms (hazard ratio [HR], 0.93; 95% CI, 0.68--1.27), and DA-EPOCH-R was associated with greater toxicity without improvement in PFS or OS [@Bartlett2019Doseadjusted]. The study concluded that the more intensive infusional regimen offered no benefit over R-CHOP in an unselected DLBCL population.

Substitution of rituximab with the glycoengineered type II anti-CD20 antibody obinutuzumab was tested in the GOYA phase III trial, which randomized 1,418 previously untreated patients to G-CHOP versus R-CHOP [@Vitolo2017Obinutuzumab]. After a median observation of 29 months, investigator-assessed PFS events were similar between arms (stratified HR, 0.92; 95% CI, 0.76--1.11; P = .39), with 3-year PFS rates of 70% and 67%, respectively [@Vitolo2017Obinutuzumab]. The addition of bortezomib to R-CHOP was evaluated in the REMoDL-B phase III trial, which incorporated prospective gene expression profiling, but similarly failed to demonstrate a PFS advantage [@Davies2019Geneexpression]. Intensification with high-dose chemotherapy and autologous stem cell transplantation (ASCT) as consolidation in high-risk young patients (age-adjusted IPI 2--3) was investigated in the DLCL04 phase III trial but did not improve outcomes over a full course of rituximab-dose-dense chemotherapy [@Chiappella2017Rituximabdosedense].

## Limited-Stage Disease

For patients with limited-stage (stage I--II) DLBCL with favorable prognostic features, de-escalation strategies have been successfully pursued. The FLYER phase III non-inferiority trial enrolled 592 patients aged 18--60 years with stage I--II disease, normal LDH, ECOG 0--1, and no bulky disease, randomizing them to six cycles of R-CHOP versus four cycles of R-CHOP plus two additional doses of rituximab [@Poeschel2019Four]. After a median follow-up of 66 months, four cycles of CHOP with six applications of rituximab was non-inferior to six cycles of R-CHOP, establishing a reduced-chemotherapy approach as standard for this favorable-risk population [@Poeschel2019Four]. For patients with limited-stage disease and adverse risk factors (bulky disease, elevated LDH), the optimal approach remains debated, with options including abbreviated chemotherapy (three to four cycles of R-CHOP) followed by involved-field radiation therapy versus a full course of six cycles of R-CHOP without radiation. The choice between these strategies depends on disease bulk, anatomic site, and patient preference, with ongoing trials seeking to clarify the role of PET-adapted approaches in this setting.

## The POLARIX Trial: Pola-R-CHP

The POLARIX trial represents the first phase III study to demonstrate a statistically significant PFS improvement over R-CHOP in previously untreated DLBCL [@Tilly2022Polatuzumab]. This double-blind, placebo-controlled, international trial randomized 879 patients with intermediate-risk or high-risk DLBCL (IPI score 2--5) to receive six cycles of pola-R-CHP (in which polatuzumab vedotin, an antibody-drug conjugate targeting CD79b, replaced vincristine) or standard R-CHOP, each followed by two cycles of rituximab alone [@Tilly2022Polatuzumab]. After a median follow-up of 28.2 months, the 2-year PFS was significantly higher in the pola-R-CHP group compared with R-CHOP (76.7% [95% CI, 72.7--80.8] vs. 70.2% [95% CI, 65.8--74.6]; stratified HR for progression, relapse, or death, 0.73; 95% CI, 0.57--0.95; P = 0.02) [@Tilly2022Polatuzumab]. However, OS at 2 years did not differ significantly between groups (88.7% vs. 88.6%; HR for death, 0.94; 95% CI, 0.65--1.37; P = 0.75), and the safety profiles were comparable [@Tilly2022Polatuzumab].

Polatuzumab vedotin is a CD79b-directed antibody conjugated to the microtubule-disrupting agent monomethyl auristatin E (MMAE). Earlier phase II data from the ROMULUS study had demonstrated its activity in relapsed/refractory DLBCL, with an objective response rate of 54% (complete response, 21%) when combined with rituximab [@Morschhauser2019Polatuzumab]. The POLARIX results led to regulatory approval of pola-R-CHP as a frontline option for intermediate- and high-risk DLBCL, representing the first meaningful modification to the R-CHOP backbone in over 20 years. Whether the PFS benefit will translate into an OS advantage with longer follow-up remains an important question, and updated analyses are eagerly anticipated. Subgroup analyses suggest that the benefit of pola-R-CHP may be most pronounced in patients with ABC-DLBCL and those with higher IPI scores, though these exploratory findings require prospective validation [@Tilly2022Polatuzumab].
